title: How Nashville Became Home to a Full-Scale Replica of the Parthenon
description:
in Architecture, History, Travel |   	July 7th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Asked to idenôÙtiôÙfy ãthe Athens of the South,ã many AmerôÙiôÙcans migh
body:
in Architecture, History, Travel |   	July 7th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Asked to idenôÙtiôÙfy ãthe Athens of the South,ã many AmerôÙiôÙcans might well point to Athens, GeorôÙgia, espeôÙcialôÙly if they hapôÙpen to be fans of REM, the Bã52s, or Of MonôÙtreôÙal. In fact, that title was claimed by Nashville, TenôÙnessee as earôÙly as the eighôÙteen-fifties, when the city put into action its ambiôÙtious plans for a pubôÙlic eduôÙcaôÙtion sysôÙtem. By the end of that cenôÙtuôÙry, Nashville boastôÙed not just more than 20 colôÙleges and uniôÙverôÙsiôÙties (VanôÙderôÙbilt being the best known today), but also a full-scale repliôÙca of the Parthenon, the ancient temôÙple to the godôÙdess Athena. It was built for the stateãs CenôÙtenôÙniôÙal ExhiôÙbiôÙtion in 1897, when no disôÙplay of local grandeur was too much.


	

NearôÙly 130 years latôÙer, the Nashville Parthenon remains a major local attracôÙtion alongôÙside the likes of the Grand Ole Opry, the CounôÙtry Music Hall of Fame, and the Honky Tonk HighôÙway. The strucôÙture curôÙrentôÙly sitôÙuôÙatôÙed in CenôÙtenôÙniôÙal Park (also the home of that modôÙern site of pilôÙgrimôÙage, the TayôÙlor Swift Bench) isnãt the same one at which visôÙiôÙtors marôÙveled in 1897.
After a couôÙple of decades of deteôÙriôÙoôÙraôÙtion, writes ArtôÙsyãs Isaac Kaplan, ãmasôÙsive renôÙoôÙvaôÙtions were underôÙtakôÙen in 1920, overôÙseen by an archiôÙtect named RusôÙsell Hart, who comôÙmitôÙted to makôÙing the buildôÙing both endurôÙing and as hisôÙtorôÙiôÙcalôÙly true to the origôÙiôÙnal Parthenon as posôÙsiôÙble,ã an extenôÙsive rebuild that even entailed makôÙing casts of the origôÙiôÙnal marôÙbles.


	

Unlike the bombed-out ruin in the Athens of Greece, the Nashville Parthenon stands proudôÙly intact. But does it pass muster with seriôÙous enthuôÙsiôÙasts of clasôÙsiôÙcal civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtion? In the video at the top of the post, GarôÙrett Ryan of ancient-hisôÙtoôÙry YouTube chanôÙnel Told in Stone makes the trip. He notes that, though it does conôÙtain a gold-platôÙed (or rather, gold-leaf platôÙed) statôÙue of Athena much like the one origôÙiôÙnalôÙly sculptôÙed by Phidias, the buildôÙing is ãnot an exact repliôÙca. Itãs made of conôÙcrete, not marôÙble, it has no frieze, the colôÙors are all wrong, and the inteôÙriôÙor is very difôÙferôÙent from the origôÙiôÙnal. But it gives a sense of the scale of the Parthenon,ã and ãcapôÙtures the expeôÙriôÙence of visôÙitôÙing a temôÙple of this size.ã The parkôÙing lot right alongôÙside it does some harm to the illuôÙsion, grantôÙed, but it does encourôÙage the visôÙiôÙtor to reflect upon the nature of civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtion: AmerôÙiôÙcan civôÙiôÙlizaôÙtion, that is.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
The City of Nashville Built a Full-Scale RepliôÙca of the Parthenon in 1897, and Itãs Still StandôÙing Today
A Tour of Athensã AcropôÙoôÙlis, Explained with 3D ReconôÙstrucôÙtions
A 3D ModôÙel Reveals What the Parthenon and Its InteôÙriôÙor Looked Like 2,500 Years Ago
How the Ancient Greeks Built Their MagôÙnifôÙiôÙcent TemôÙples: The Art of Ancient EngiôÙneerôÙing
A VirôÙtuôÙal Tour of Ancient Athens: Fly Over ClasôÙsiôÙcal Greek CivôÙiôÙlizaôÙtion in All Its GloôÙry
How the Parthenon MarôÙbles EndôÙed Up In The British MuseôÙum
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Benedict Cumberbatch Reads Albert Camusã Touching Thank You Letter to His Elementary School Teacher
description:
Itãs nevôÙer too late to thank the teacher who changed your life.
Oprah WinôÙfrey fell to pieces when she was reunitôÙed on air with Mrs. DunôÙca
body:
Itãs nevôÙer too late to thank the teacher who changed your life.
Oprah WinôÙfrey fell to pieces when she was reunitôÙed on air with Mrs. DunôÙcan, her fourth grade teacher, her ãfirst libôÙerôÙaôÙtorã and ãvalôÙidaôÙtor.ã
Patrick StewôÙart used his knightôÙhood cerôÙeôÙmoôÙny as an occaôÙsion to thank Cecil DorôÙmand, the EngôÙlish teacher who told him that Shakespeareãs works were not draôÙmatôÙic poems, but plays to be perôÙformed on oneãs feet.
And Bill Gates had kind words for Blanche Caffiere, the forôÙmer librarôÙiôÙan at View Ridge EleôÙmenôÙtary in SeatôÙtle, who desôÙtigôÙmaôÙtized his role as a ãmessy, nerdy boy who was readôÙing lots of books.ã
One of the most heartôÙfelt stuôÙdent-to-teacher tribôÙutes is that of Nobel Prize-winôÙning author and philosoôÙpher Albert Camus to Louis GerôÙmain, a father subôÙstiôÙtute whose classôÙroom was a welôÙcome reprieve from the extreme poverôÙty Camus expeôÙriôÙenced at home. GerôÙmain perôÙsuadôÙed Camusã widôÙowed mothôÙer to allow Camus to comôÙpete for the scholôÙarôÙship that enabledô him to attend high school.
As read aloud by actor BeneôÙdict CumôÙberôÙbatch, above, at LetôÙters Live, a ãcelôÙeôÙbraôÙtion of the endurôÙing powôÙer of litôÙerôÙary corôÙreôÙsponôÙdence,ã Camusã 1957 mesôÙsage to GerôÙmain is an exerôÙcise in humilôÙiôÙty and simôÙply statôÙed gratôÙiôÙtude:
Dear MonôÙsieur GerôÙmain,
I let the comôÙmoôÙtion around me these days subôÙside a bit before speakôÙing to you from the botôÙtom of my heart. I have just been givôÙen far too great an honôÙour, one I neiôÙther sought nor solicitôÙed.
But when I heard the news, my first thought, after my mothôÙer, was of you. WithôÙout you, withôÙout the affecôÙtionôÙate hand you extendôÙed to the small poor child that I was, withôÙout your teachôÙing and examôÙple, none of all this would have hapôÙpened.
I donãt make too much of this sort of honôÙour. But at least it gives me the opporôÙtuôÙniôÙty to tell you what you have been and still are for me, and to assure you that your efforts, your work, and the genôÙerôÙous heart you put into it still live in one of your litôÙtle schoolôÙboys who, despite the years, has nevôÙer stopped being your grateôÙful pupil. I embrace you with all my heart.
Albert Camus
The letôÙter was grateôÙfulôÙly received by his forôÙmer teacher, who wrote back a year and a half latôÙer to say in part:
If it were posôÙsiôÙble, I would squeeze the great boy whom you have become, and who will always remain for me ãmy litôÙtle Camus.ã
He comôÙpliôÙmentôÙed his litôÙtle Camus on not letôÙting fame go to his head, and urged him to conôÙtinôÙue makôÙing his famôÙiôÙly a priôÙorôÙiôÙty. He shared some fond memôÙoôÙries of Camus as a genôÙtle, optiôÙmistic, intelôÙlecôÙtuôÙalôÙly curiôÙous litôÙtle felôÙlow, and praised his mothôÙer for doing her best in difôÙfiôÙcult cirôÙcumôÙstances.
ReadôÙers, please use the comôÙments secôÙtion to share with us the teachôÙers deservôÙing of your thanks.
You can find this letôÙter, and many more, in the great LetôÙters of Note book.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2017.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
Albert Camus: The MadôÙness of SinôÙcerôÙiôÙty ã 1997 DocôÙuôÙmenôÙtary RevisôÙits the Philosopherãs Life & Work
Albert Camus, EdiôÙtor of the French ResisôÙtance NewsôÙpaôÙper ComôÙbat, Writes MovôÙingôÙly About Life, PolôÙiôÙtics & War (1944ã47)
Hear Albert Camus DelivôÙer His Nobel Prize AccepôÙtance Speech (1957)
Ayun HalôÙlôÙiôÙdayô is an author, illusôÙtraôÙtor, theôÙater makôÙer in NYC.

title: How an Edward Hopper Painting Inspired Norman Batesã Iconic House in Alfred Hitchcockãs Psycho
description:
in Art, Film, History |   	July 8th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Alfred HitchôÙcock was not AmerôÙiôÙcan, as even casuôÙal viewôÙers of his teleôÙviôÙsion
body:
in Art, Film, History |   	July 8th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			
Alfred HitchôÙcock was not AmerôÙiôÙcan, as even casuôÙal viewôÙers of his teleôÙviôÙsion show could tell right away. He may have exagôÙgerôÙatôÙed his EngôÙlishôÙness, but like more than a few high-proôÙfile outôÙsiders, he also used his culôÙturôÙal posiôÙtion to renôÙder the UnitôÙed States all the more vividôÙly in his work. GrowôÙing up, he amassed enough secôÙond-hand knowlôÙedge of the counôÙtry in which he would one day live that he already knew his way around New York when first he set foot there. But it was some years after he reloôÙcatôÙed to HolôÙlyôÙwood that his films began to feel AmerôÙiôÙcan ã and, evenôÙtuôÙalôÙly, more AmerôÙiôÙcan than those made by domesôÙtic direcôÙtors, thanks in part to his unconôÙvenôÙtionôÙal perôÙspecôÙtive on local sources of inspiôÙraôÙtion.

Image by Diego DelôÙso, via WikiôÙmeôÙdia ComôÙmons
Take the archiôÙtecôÙture. Asked by FranûÏois TrufôÙfaut about NorôÙman Batesã ãghostôÙly houseã in PsyôÙcho, he explained that ãthe mysôÙteôÙriôÙous atmosôÙphere is, to some extent, quite acciôÙdenôÙtal. For instance, the actuôÙal locale of the events is in northôÙern CaliôÙfornia, where that type of house is very comôÙmon.ã He wasôÙnãt tryôÙing to ãreconôÙstruct an old-fashôÙioned UniôÙverôÙsal horôÙror picôÙture atmosôÙphere,ã but ãsimôÙply wantôÙed to be accuôÙrate.ã Yet the house is reportôÙed to have been inspired by an east-coast modôÙel as well, and one found in art: Edward HopôÙperãs paintôÙingHouse by the RailôÙroad(top), from 1925, itself made with refôÙerôÙence to a real VicôÙtoôÙriôÙan manôÙsion that still stands in HaverôÙstraw, New York, between a railôÙroad and a cemeôÙtery.
HitchôÙcock had already made use of HopôÙper, that most cinôÙeôÙmatôÙic of AmerôÙiôÙcan painters. Here on Open CulôÙture, weãve preôÙviôÙousôÙly feaôÙtured the visuôÙal influôÙence of HopôÙper paintôÙings from the nineôÙteen-twenôÙties and thirôÙties like Automat, Night WinôÙdows, Hotel Room, and Room in New York on Rear WinôÙdow. ãBoth artists explored the loneôÙliôÙness that results from modôÙernôÙizaôÙtion,ã writes Tim Brinkhof at ArtôÙnet. ãHopperãs paintôÙings and HitchôÙcockôÙãs films explore the extent to which progress and urban modôÙernôÙizaôÙtion have made the world loneôÙliôÙer and, as a result, capaôÙble of acts of exploôÙsive, irraôÙtional vioôÙlence,ã a capaôÙbilôÙiôÙty perôÙsonôÙiôÙfied in the disôÙturbed motel-keepôÙer NorôÙman Bates.

ãThe [HaverôÙstraw] house was built in 1885, near the crest of a hill that risôÙes steeply from the west bank of the HudôÙson RivôÙer,ã writes Paul BochnôÙer in the Atlantic. ãBy the turn of the cenôÙtuôÙry it had been abanôÙdoned; neighôÙborôÙhood chilôÙdren called it hauntôÙed.ã It was latôÙer purôÙchased by the disôÙtrict attorôÙney of RockôÙland CounôÙty, whose eldest daughôÙter rememôÙbered that, ãwhen she was thirôÙteen, she looked out her bedôÙroom winôÙdow and saw a man sitôÙting across the road, paintôÙing.ã The man was, of course, Edward HopôÙper. She wouldôÙnãt have known, sevôÙenôÙteen years before Nighthawks, that he was on his way to becomôÙing one of the counôÙtryãs most famous artists. As for what the house would one day become in the hands of Alfred HitchôÙcock, then just startôÙing his career on the othôÙer side of the Atlantic, nobody could have imagôÙined.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
16 Free HitchôÙcock Movies Online
How Edward Hopperãs PaintôÙings Inspired the Creepy SusôÙpense of Alfred Hitchcockãs Rear WinôÙdow
How CinôÙeôÙma Inspired Edward Hopperãs Great PaintôÙings, and How Edward HopôÙper Inspired Great FilmôÙmakôÙers
Alfred HitchôÙcock WantôÙed Frank Lloyd Wright to Design the North by NorthôÙwest House: An ArchiôÙtect Just Built It for $45 MilôÙlion
How Edward HopôÙper ãStoôÙryôÙboardôÙedã His IconôÙic PaintôÙing Nighthawks
SalôÙvador DalûÙ Goes to HolôÙlyôÙwood & CreôÙates a Wild Dream Sequence for Alfred HitchôÙcock
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: A 3,000-Year-Old Painterãs Palette from Ancient Egypt, with Traces of the Original Colors Still In It
description: Itãs a good bet that your first box of crayons or waterôÙcolôÙors was a simôÙple affair of six or so colôÙorsãÎ just like the palette belongôÙing to Am
body:
Itãs a good bet that your first box of crayons or waterôÙcolôÙors was a simôÙple affair of six or so colôÙorsãÎ just like the palette belongôÙing to AmenôÙemopet, vizier to Pharaoh AmenôÙhotep III (c.1391 ã c.1354 BC), a pleaôÙsure-lovôÙing patron of the arts whose rule coinôÙcidôÙed with a periôÙod of great prosôÙperôÙiôÙty.
Amenemopetãs well-usedô artistãs palette, above, resides in the EgyptôÙian wing of New York Cityãs MetôÙroôÙpolôÙiôÙtan MuseôÙum of Art.
Over 3000 years old and carved from a sinôÙgle piece of ivory, the palette is marked ãbeloved of Re,ã a royôÙal refôÙerôÙence to the sun god dear to both AmenôÙhotep III andô AkhenôÙaton,ô his son and sucôÙcesôÙsor, whose worôÙship of Re resemôÙbled monotheôÙism.
As curaôÙtor Catharine H. Roehrig notes in the MetôÙroôÙpolôÙiôÙtanãs pubôÙliôÙcaôÙtion,ô Life along the Nile: Three EgypôÙtians of Ancient Thebes, the palette ãconôÙtains the six basic colôÙors of the EgyptôÙian palette, plus two extras: redôÙdish brown, a mixôÙture of red ocher and carôÙbon; and orange, a mixôÙture of orpiôÙment (yelôÙlow) and red ocher. The painter could also vary his colôÙors by applyôÙing a thickôÙer or thinôÙner layôÙer of paint or by adding white or black to achieve a lighter or darkôÙer shade.ã
(CareôÙful when mixôÙing that orpiôÙment into your red ocher, kids. Itãs a form of arsenic.)
OthôÙer minôÙerôÙals that would have been ground and comôÙbined with a natôÙurôÙal bindôÙing agent include gypôÙsum, carôÙbon, iron oxides, blue and green azuôÙrite and malaôÙchite.

The colôÙors themôÙselves would have had strong symôÙbolôÙism for AmenôÙhotep and his peoôÙple, and the artist would have made very delibôÙerôÙateãregôÙuôÙlatôÙed, evenãchoicôÙes as to which pigôÙment to load onto hisô palm fiberô brush when decôÙoôÙratôÙing tombs, temôÙples, pubôÙlic buildôÙings, and potôÙtery.
As JenôÙny Hill writes inô Ancient Egypt Online,ô iwnãcolôÙorãcan also be transôÙlatôÙed as ãdisôÙpoôÙsiôÙtion,ã ãcharôÙacôÙter,ã ãcomôÙplexôÙion,ã or ãnature.ã She delves into the specifics of each of the six basic colôÙors:
Wadj (green)ô also means ãto flourôÙishã or ãto be healthy.ã The hieroôÙglyph repôÙreôÙsentôÙed the papyrus plant as well as the green stone malaôÙchite (wadj). The colôÙor green repôÙreôÙsentôÙed vegôÙeôÙtaôÙtion, new life and ferôÙtilôÙiôÙty. In an interôÙestôÙing parôÙalôÙlel with modôÙern terôÙmiôÙnolôÙoôÙgy, actions which preôÙserved the ferôÙtilôÙiôÙty of the land or proôÙmotôÙed life were described as ãgreen.ã
Dshr (red)ô was a powôÙerôÙful colôÙor because of its assoôÙciôÙaôÙtion with blood, in parôÙticôÙuôÙlar the proôÙtecôÙtive powôÙer of the blood ofô IsisãÎred could also repôÙreôÙsent anger, chaos and fire and was closeôÙly assoôÙciôÙatôÙed withô Set, the unpreôÙdictable god of storms. Set had red hair, and peoôÙple with red hair were thought to be conôÙnectôÙed to him.ô As a result, the EgypôÙtians described a perôÙson in a fit of rage as havôÙing a ãred heartã or as being ãred uponã the thing that made them angry. A perôÙson was described as havôÙing ãred eyesã if they were angry or vioôÙlent. ãTo redôÙdenã was to die and ãmakôÙing redã was a euphemism for killing.
Irtyu (blue)ô was the colôÙor of the heavôÙens and hence repôÙreôÙsentôÙed the uniôÙverse. Many temôÙples, sarôÙcophaôÙgi and burôÙial vaults have a deep blue roof speckôÙled with tiny yelôÙlow stars. Blue is also the colôÙor of the Nile and the primeval waters of chaos (known asô Nun).
Khenet (yelôÙlow)ô repôÙreôÙsentôÙed that which was eterôÙnal and indeôÙstrucôÙtible, and was closeôÙly assoôÙciôÙatôÙed with gold (nebu or nebw) and the sun. Gold was thought to be the subôÙstance which formed the skin of the gods.
Hdj (white)ô repôÙreôÙsentôÙed puriôÙty and omnipoôÙtence. Many sacred aniôÙmals (hipôÙpo, oxen and cows) were white. White clothôÙing was worn durôÙing reliôÙgious ritôÙuôÙals and to ãwear white sanôÙdalsã was to be a priestãÎWhite was also seen as the oppoôÙsite of red, because of the latterãs assoôÙciôÙaôÙtion with rage and chaos, and so the two were often paired to repôÙreôÙsent comôÙpleteôÙness.
Kem (black)ô repôÙreôÙsentôÙed death and the afterôÙlife to the ancient EgypôÙtians.ô Osirisô was givôÙen the epiôÙthet ãthe black oneã because he was the king of the netherôÙworld, and both he andô AnuôÙbisô (the god of embalmôÙing) were porôÙtrayed with black faces. The EgypôÙtians also assoôÙciôÙatôÙed black with ferôÙtilôÙiôÙty and resôÙurôÙrecôÙtion because much of their agriôÙculôÙture was depenôÙdent on the rich dark silt depositôÙed on the rivôÙer banks by the Nile durôÙing the inunôÙdaôÙtion. When used to repôÙreôÙsent resôÙurôÙrecôÙtion, black and green were interôÙchangeôÙable.

If you would like to supôÙport the misôÙsion of Open CulôÙture, conôÙsidôÙer makôÙingô a donaôÙtion to our site. Itãs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your conôÙtriôÙbuôÙtions will help us conôÙtinôÙue proôÙvidôÙing the best free culôÙturôÙal and eduôÙcaôÙtionôÙal mateôÙriôÙals to learnôÙers everyôÙwhere. You can conôÙtribute through PayôÙPal, PatreôÙon, and VenôÙmo (@openculture). Thanks!
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2021.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:ô 
WonôÙders of Ancient Egypt: A Free Online Course from the UniôÙverôÙsiôÙty of PennôÙsylôÙvaôÙnia
Harvardãs DigôÙiôÙtal Giza Project Lets You Access the Largest Online Archive on the EgyptôÙian PyraôÙmids (IncludôÙing a 3D Giza Tour)
PyraôÙmids of Giza: Ancient EgyptôÙian Art and Archaeologyãa Free Online Course from HarôÙvard

title: Collin van der Sluijs' 'Wanderland' Imagines a Vast Ecosystem Encompassing the Momentous and Mundane
description: Joy Machine andô Vertical Galleryô are excited to share their first collaborative exhibition,ô Wanderlandô byô Collin van der Sluijs. This marks the ar
body:
Joy Machine andô Vertical Galleryô are excited to share their first collaborative exhibition,ô Wanderlandô byô Collin van der Sluijs. This marks the artistãs sixth solo exhibition in Chicago and more than a decade in partnership with Vertical Gallery. Itãs his first presentation at Joy Machine.
Wanderlandô comprises a new body of work developed during a two-year period. Spanning enormous mixed-media paintings, intimate drawings, and skate decks bearing the artistãs signature birds, the exhibition reflects both the momentous and mundane, particularly loss, grief, and the therapeutic powers of nature.
ãWanderlandã (2026), mixed media on paper, framed
A portmanteau of wander and wonderland, the title evokes van der Sluijsã continued devotion to imagining a vast, dreamlike ecosystem. For the past decade, heãs produced various bodies of work as a sort of world-building exercise, intuitively selecting a blend of oil paint, acrylic, watercolor, and more to render dynamic still lifes and portraits of figures subsumed by chaos. Wanderland continues in this vein, once again presenting hazy bouquets that allude to the artistãs Dutch heritage and a melange of symbols and markings referencing his background in street art. ãCollin works in such a wide range of stylesãlike a modern twist on classical painting, with graffiti and illustration mixed in,ã Patrick Hull, Verticalãs owner and curator, shares.
Each collection emerges through a lengthy process of trial and error, and often, van der Sluijs will work on a piece for years, even turning it around to face the wall periodically to provide a brief respite while in his studio. ãI really need to make big mistakes and fight my way out of it,ã he adds. In this way, cultivating wonder and an unceasing desire to explore his inner emotional and external landscapes is an essential part of the process.ô ô Wanderlandô is about ãa walk to the visual world that you create around yourself, like wandering around. But itãs also like diving into the unknown.ã
Following a collection of darker pieces made during the early years of the pandemic,ô Wanderlandô emerges with more levity and hope, albeit not without difficulty and grief. While working toward the exhibition, the artist broke his ankle, rendering it impossible for him to stand while painting larger canvases hanging on the wall. Instead, he had to sit, stretching himself across the horizontal surfaces and contending with a novel, challenging mode of making.
And despite his physical limitations, van der Sluijs also spent more time venturing outdoors during the last two years. Making small works on paper amid natural settings was a sort of balm in processing the recent death of a friend. ãAll those personal stories, they make the context of a show,ã he adds.
ãFloater #2ã (2026), mixed media on canvas, 7 x 9.4 inches
To celebrate more than a decade of collaborations, Vertical Gallery published a book commemorating the occasions titledô Wanderland: 1991-2026.ô Its pages juxtapose the artistãs exploits as a painter, muralist, and graffiti writer with the more personal moments that define his roles as husband, son, father, and friend.ô 
Wanderlandô runs from July 10 to August 22.ô An opening receptionô will be held from 6 to 9 p.m. on July 10. The artist will be present.
ãWhite Line Sphinx Moth drawingã (2026), mixed media on paper, framed, 8.3 x 11.7 inches
ãLoose Lips Sink Shipsã (2026), oil on canvas, 72 x 82 inches
ãBird study #2ã (2026), mixed media on paper, framed, 14.2 x 18.7 inches
ãTranslated Watercolor XL #2ã (2026), acrylic, ink, pencil, and oilsticks on canvas, 53 x 76 inches

schema.headline: Collin van der Sluijs' 'Wanderland' Imagines a Vast Ecosystem Encompassing the Momentous and Mundane

title: Atmospheric Scenes and Layered Interpretations Top the 2026 Hasselblad Masters Contest
description: In Pioche, Nevada, a movie theater built in 1937 stands in tribute to the immense changes in technology and style over nearly a century. A bold po
body:
In Pioche, Nevada, a movie theater built in 1937 stands in tribute to the immense changes in technology and style over nearly a century. A bold portrait set against a black background by photographer Kevin Boyle captures not only the aging building but a mid-20th-century pastime that taps into nostalgia and small town identity. (Amid preservation efforts, the theater is currently being rehabilitated.) Itãs just one of the works in the artistãs ongoing Movieland series, which won the Architecture category in this yearãs Hasselblad Masters 2026 contest.
Additional categories in the prestigious competition include Wildlife, Landscape, Art, Portrait, Street, and Project//21, which highlights work by entrants aged 21 or younger. This year, photographers in 160 countries submitted more than 108,000 images, which were narrowed down to 70 finalists.
Svetlana Jovanovic, from ãOthernessã
In addition to their technical aptitude, the artists illuminate lesser-known pockets of the world by examining distinctive social issues, nature, and geographies. The winning photos are atmospheric and even surreal, such as Rohan Reillyãs low-exposure Ephemeral Visions series of rows of trees and Gosse Boumaãs misty depictions of Netherlandish market stalls in Morning Ritual.
Svetlana Jovanovic turns her lens to bold dual portraits of identical twins, and Alfred Minnaarãs otherworldly captures of underwater vitality topped the Wildlife category. Panitbhand Paribatra Na Ayudhya, winner of the Project//21 category, is a 14-year-old scuba diver from Thailand who explores the beauty, fragility, and biodiversity of the ocean in beautiful portrayals of marine life.
Hasselblad Foundation executive director Kalle Sanner says, ãAcross categories, the strongest work operated on more than one level simultaneously: legible on first encounter, yet resistant to easy interpretation. These are images that require attention, that continue to unfold the longer you stay with them.ã For example, Yudha Kusuma Puteraãs uncanny photographs of huddled cowsã backs nod to the unseen. What we donãt see is as significant as what we do, as these animals are actually grazing on heaps of trash in a dump on the outskirts of Yogyakarta, Indonesia. Exploring notions of labor and visibility, Putera takes a visually literal approach to ãout of sight, out of mind.ã
See all the winning images on the Hasselblad Masters website.
Yudha Kusuma Putera, from ãWaste Colonialism (Sapi-Sapi Piyungan)ã
Grosse Bouma, from ãMorning Ritualã
Alfred Minnaar, from ãThe Forest I Roamã
Kevin Boyle, from ãDaySleeper | Movielandã
Yudha Kusuma Putera, from ãWaste Colonialism (Sapi-Sapi Piyungan)ã
Rohan Reilly, from ãEphemeral Visionsã
Panitbhand Paribatra Na Ayudhya, from ãDwellers of the Nightã
Svetlana Jovanovic, from ãOthernessã
Alfred Minnaar, from ãThe Forest I Roamã
Grosse Bouma, from ãMorning Ritualã

title: Amelia Cross Blends Bespoke Tailoring and Trompe-L'éil Painting in Her Sartorial Studies
description: Although Amelia Cross subscribes to the belief that our sartorial choices are a way to signal who we are and what we care about, she also knows th
body:
Although Amelia Cross subscribes to the belief that our sartorial choices are a way to signal who we are and what we care about, she also knows there are more subliminal details hiding in personal style. A nametag curling at the edges or a pen bleeding through a shirt pocket stand in stark contrast to a perfectly pressed collar or shiny brogues, but each also has the potential to conceal or obscure. These covert elements are what the London-born artist is most interested in unpacking.
After receiving a degree in bespoke tailoring at the London College of Fashion, followed by a masterãs in painting at the Royal College of Art, Cross began to meld the two disciplines. ãI originally painted figureless compositions of clothing on a flat linen surface but felt my hands itching to use my sewing machine alongside painting. I started making small studies of sewn pockets and structured collars with painted objects and buttons, and I found this odd juxtaposition of the real and painted shadows really intriguing,ã she said.
ãThe Ice-breakerã (2026)
The artist landed on the concept of ãsewn paintings,ã which combine trompe-lãéil methods with constructed, sculptural details. Illusory in technique and legible in subject matter, the works prompt the viewer to decipher which three-dimensional components are real.
Cross stretches her own canvases and sometimes thinks about the wooden armature as a body, one sheãs able to dress and style. More focused works hone in on a singular pocket or pair of shoes. Many of her ideas, though, emerge while observing other commuters on the London Underground. She explains:

During rush hour, youãre often packed in a carriage like sardines, which is sometimes why my paintings have a slim tight crop as I can only see a partial element of clothing at a time. You can almost build up a whole character for someone based on whatãs inside their pocket or what socks they are wearing to work!

For her solo exhibition Discipline and Display at Nino Mier Gallery, Cross leans into dress codes, uniforms, and the overt or more subconscious rules that inform how we put together an outfit. The hidden agendas and social structures that inform fashion appear in the artistãs compositions, too.
ãThe Britã (2026)
ãI often conceal secrets or confessions in the works (some on display and some totally hidden) such as painted inner collar labels, messages inside pockets and buttons on the sides and undersides of paintings,ã she adds. The goal is to entice the viewer to get up close and personal with the disembodied forms, offering the opportunity to study the minute details and signals we might otherwise overlook.
If youãre in New York, Discipline and Display is on view through August 7. Otherwise, find more from Cross on Instagram.
ãThe Pairã (2026)
ãThe Compromiseã (2026)
ãThe Miscreantã (2026)
ãCowboyã (2026)
Detail of ãThe Ice-breakerã (2026)
